1:
"There were present at that season some that told him of the Galilaeans, whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices." The witnesses dwell on the report, the slain Galileans, and the motive of those who brought it.

The occasion arose from Christ's preaching. The Gloss notes that "as he had been speaking of the punishments of sinners, the story is fitly told him of the punishment of certain particular sinners, from which he takes occasion to denounce vengeance also against other sinners."

Cyril supplies the background. These Galileans "were followers of the opinions of Judas of Galilee" (cf. Acts 5:37), who "refused to acknowledge Caesar as their master," and forbade the sacrifices offered for the Emperor; "Pilate then, being enraged, ordered them to be slain in the midst of the very victims," so that "the blood of the offerers was mingled with that of the victims." The report was not innocent: "the rulers, eager to excite against him the hatred of the people, relate these things to the Saviour, wishing to discover what he thought."

Christ's answer the fathers anticipate. He "admits them to be sinners," Cyril notes, but "does not judge them to have suffered such things as though they were worse than those who suffered not"; rather he turns it to a call: "unless ye repent... ye shall all likewise perish." Bede sees the warning fulfilled: because the nation repented not, "the Romans, beginning from Galilee, utterly destroyed that wicked nation, and defiled with human blood... the courts of the temples." The witnesses thus see the scene open with a piece of grim news, brought perhaps to test or to ensnare; and Christ, refusing both to excuse Pilate and to condemn the slain as peculiarly guilty, makes the tragedy a mirror in which all his hearers are bidden to see their own peril and need of repentance. Chrysostom adds that God in such judgments "permitted them to suffer such things, that the heirs of the kingdom yet living might be dismayed by the dangers of others" — not that the slain were punished for the sake of the survivors, but that their fall becomes, to those who see it, an occasion of salvation.
2:
"And Jesus answering said unto them, Suppose ye that these Galilaeans were sinners above all the Galilaeans, because they suffered such things?" The witnesses dwell on the false inference Christ corrects and the practical turn he gives.

The report assumed a special judgment. Barnes: "it would appear that they supposed that the fact that these men had been slain in this manner proved that they were very great sinners." Ellicott: "the tale had probably been told with a conviction... that the massacre had been a special judgment for some special and exceptional guilt." Christ "at once sweeps away all their rash interpretations of the divine government" (cf. John 9:2).

The principle the commentators draw out is large. Barnes: "the fact that men come to a sudden and violent death is not proof that they are peculiarly wicked"; "this is not a world of retribution. Good and evil are mingled; the good and the bad suffer." Often "the most wicked are suffered to prosper here... while the righteous are called to suffer much" (cf. Job 4:7). There is "another world — a future state — where all that is irregular on earth will be regulated."

Yet Christ turns the matter inward. Barnes: "he never suffered a suitable occasion to pass without warning the wicked... The subject of religion was always present to his mind." The reproof was "delicate and yet severe": "he did not tell them that they were as bad as the Galileans, but left them to infer it, for if they did not repent, they must soon likewise be destroyed." The witnesses thus see Christ correcting a deep human error — the rush to read others' calamities as proof of peculiar guilt — and replacing the idle speculation with a searching personal question: not "how wicked were they?" but "are you ready to die?" Ellicott notes that here, as in the case of the man born blind (John 9:3), our Lord refuses to let suffering be read as the simple wages of a particular sin; the rush to such verdicts, seen in Job's friends, is precisely what Christ forbids, bidding men look not at the calamity of others but at their own standing before God.
3:
"I tell you, Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish." The witnesses dwell on the denial, the call to repent, and its terrible fulfilment.

The Galileans were not the chief of sinners. Gill: "they were not greater sinners than others of their neighbours... others might be much more deserving of such an end than they, who yet escaped it." Their fate was no measure of their guilt.

The call to repent is pressing and continual. Robertson notes "except ye repent" is the present active subjunctive — "to change mind and conduct, linear action, keep on changing" — a condition "with prospect of determination." Repentance is not a single act soon over but a turning that must continue.

"Ye shall all likewise perish" the witnesses take as a precise prophecy. Wesley: "all ye of Galilee and of Jerusalem shall perish in the very same manner. So the Greek word implies. And so they did." He marks "a remarkable resemblance between the fate of these Galileans and of the main body of the Jewish nation; the flower of which was slain at Jerusalem by the Roman sword, while they were assembled at one of their great festivals," and "many thousands of them perished in the temple itself, and were literally buried under its ruins." The witnesses thus see Christ pressing the lesson home with awful exactness. The very manner of the Galileans' death — slain at their sacrifices, their blood mingled with the offerings — would be the manner of the nation's doom if it did not repent. The warning was no vague threat but a foretelling of A.D. 70, when impenitent Jerusalem would perish, as these had, amid its feasts and sacrifices. Repentance alone could avert it — for the nation then, and for every soul that hears the call (cf. 2 Peter 3:9). Robertson notes the moral Christ draws is plain even though the interpretation of accidents is hard: the lesson is not to explain the disaster but to heed the summons; every such event is a call to the living, not a sentence read over the dead.
4:
"Or those eighteen, upon whom the tower in Siloam fell, and slew them, think ye that they were sinners above all men that dwelt in Jerusalem?" The witnesses dwell on the second example, the place, and the term "sinners."

Christ himself adds a parallel case. Barnes notes he "adds another similar case, to warn them — a case which had probably occurred not long before," and "the manner in which he refers to it implies that it was fresh in the recollection of those to whom he spoke."

The tower stood at Siloam. Barnes locates the fountain "on the south side of Mount Moriah," with its pool (cf. John 9:7; Nehemiah 3:15). Lightfoot suggests "this tower might be built over the porticoes of the pool, and might overwhelm those eighteen men, while they were busied about purifying themselves," or "expecting to be healed at the troubling of the waters." Ellicott adds the intriguing possibility that, "connected as Siloam was with the water-system of the city, the tower was part of the works which Pilate had planned... for the construction of an aqueduct," for which "he appropriated part of the Corban."

The term "sinners" the commentators distinguish. Clarke notes that of these Jerusalem men the word is "debtors" (opheiletai), "a Jewish phrase for sinners" — those "bound by the law" who, failing to obey, are "reckoned debtors to the law"; whereas of the Galileans the word was hamartōloi, often used of heathen. The witnesses thus see Christ pressing the same lesson from a different kind of death. Where the Galileans fell by human cruelty, these eighteen fell by sheer accident — a tower's collapse. Yet the lesson is one: their death was no proof that they were worse than their neighbours. Whether men die by the sword of a tyrant or the fall of a tower, the survivors have no warrant to think themselves safer or better; the only safe response to any man's sudden death is to look to one's own readiness before God. Lightfoot adds that the eighteen may have perished "while they were busied about purifying themselves" at the pool, or "expecting to be healed at the troubling of the waters", which makes their sudden death the more striking — cut off in the very act of seeking cleansing or cure, with no warning at all.
5:
"I tell you, Nay: but, except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish." The witnesses dwell on the repeated warning and its fulfilment.

The denial and the call are repeated, driving the lesson deep. Gill: "they were not greater sinners than others: though such a melancholy accident befell them, not without the providence of God." Barnes presses the principle: "it is improper to suppose that those on whom heavy judgments fall in this world are the worst of men. This is not a world of retribution"; the wicked often "prosper here... while the righteous are called to suffer" — yet "the wicked will not always escape; God is just," and "in the future world all that seems unequal here will be made equal."

Robertson notes a slight change in the verb. Here "except ye repent" is the first aorist subjunctive — "immediate repentance, in contrast to continued repentance" in verse 3. The two together urge both a decisive turning and a settled change of life.

The fulfilment Clarke sets out from Josephus. "This prediction of our Lord was literally fulfilled. When the city was taken by the Romans, multitudes of the priests... who were going on with their sacrifices, were slain, and their blood mingled with the blood of their victims; and multitudes were buried under the ruins of the walls, houses, and temple." He draws the purpose of such judgments: God uses them "to alarm others... to show his hatred to sin... and to give sinners... a general specimen of the punishment that awaits all the perseveringly impenitent." The witnesses thus see Christ closing the lesson as he began it — refusing to read the two disasters as marks of peculiar guilt, and turning both into one urgent summons. The fall of a tower and the sword of Pilate alike preach the same sermon: all are sinners, all must die, and only repentance can save any from a perishing far more dreadful than either (cf. Matthew 24:2). Robertson notes that while "the interpretation of accidents is a difficult matter", the moral pointed out by Jesus is obvious: the survivor's business is not to judge the dead but to repent himself, lest a like or worse end overtake him unprepared.
6:
"He spake also this parable; A certain man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard; and he came and sought fruit thereon, and found none." The witnesses dwell on the occasion, the meaning of the fig tree, and the owner's search for fruit.

The parable answers the people's presumption. Titus of Bostra: "the Jews were boasting that, while the eighteen had perished, they all remained unhurt. He therefore sets before them the parable of the fig tree." Henry binds it to the warning just uttered: "This parable is intended to enforce that word of warning immediately going before, Except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish; except you be reformed, you will be ruined, as the barren tree, except it bring forth fruit, will be cut down." Gill likewise takes it as spoken at the same time and upon the same occasion, setting forth "the patience of God towards the Jewish nation, their unfruitfulness, and the danger of their being destroyed, in case of non-amendment."

The fig tree the fathers chiefly read as the synagogue. Ambrose: "the comparison of the fig tree to the synagogue is well chosen, because as that tree abounds with wide and spreading foliage, and deceives the hopes of its possessor with the vain expectation of promised fruit, so also in the synagogue, while its teachers are unfruitful in good works, yet magnify themselves with words as with abundant leaves." Others read it more widely — Augustine, "the race of mankind," recalling Adam who "concealed with fig leaves his nakedness" (cf. Genesis 3:7); Theophylact, "each one of us... planted in the vineyard of God, that is, in the Church, or in the world." Ambrose knows this wider reading too — some think the fig tree a figure "not of the synagogue, but of wickedness and treachery" — yet judges that these "differ in nothing from what has gone before, except that they choose the genus instead of the species."

The owner came seeking fruit, and found none. Bede: "the Lord himself, who established the synagogue by Moses, came born in the flesh, and... sought for the fruits of faith, but in the hearts of the Pharisees found none." Yet the seeking was no ignorance; Ambrose: "our Lord sought, not because He was ignorant that the fig tree had no fruit, but that He might show in a figure that the synagogue ought by this time to have fruit." He marks the threefold coming: "he came to Abraham, he came to Moses, he came to Mary... Circumcision purified, the law sanctified, grace justified," yet the people, having "the circumcision of the body" not "of the heart," and "ignorant of the meaning of the law," bore no fruit. Gregory widens the search to the whole race: the Lord "sought after man's nature before the law, under the law, and under grace, by waiting, admonishing, visiting; but yet he complains that for three years he found no fruit, for there are some wicked men whose hearts are neither corrected by the law of nature breathed into them, nor instructed by precepts, nor converted by the miracles of his incarnation." The witnesses thus see the parable as a solemn picture of Israel's privilege and barrenness: planted in God's own vineyard, tended through the ages, visited at last by the Lord himself — and found, for all its leaves, without fruit (cf. Isaiah 5:1).
7:
"Then said he unto the dresser of his vineyard, Behold, these three years I come seeking fruit on this fig tree, and find none: cut it down; why cumbereth it the ground?" The witnesses dwell on the owner's patience, the "three years," and the charge against the tree.

The dresser the commentators identify variously. Gill: "if by the owner of the vineyard is meant God the Father, then by the dresser... Jesus Christ is intended"; or the dresser may be "the ministers of the word, to whom Christ... lets out his vineyard to dress." The "three years" Barnes takes not as an exact reckoning but as showing "the owner had been a long time expecting fruit... long enough to show that the tree was barren"; Clarke, however, finds in it a hint that Christ "had been... exercising his ministry for three years past."

"Why cumbereth it the ground?" names the tree's offence. Barnes: "the word cumber here means to render barren or sterile. By taking up the juices of the earth, this useless tree rendered the ground sterile" — "it was not merely useless, but was doing mischief, which may be said of all sinners and all hypocritical professors." He adds Thomson's testimony from the land itself: "there are many such trees now; and if the ground is not properly cultivated... they do not bear at all"; those who expect good crops of figs "not only plough and dig about them frequently, and manure them plentifully," but gather the very stones out of their orchards. Lightfoot notes the law forbade lightly cutting down a fruit-bearing tree (cf. Deuteronomy 20:19), which makes the sentence the more weighty. Clarke notes that one ancient copy adds "Bring the axe and cut it down" — an allusion, doubtless, to the Baptist's warning that now the axe lieth at the root of the trees (Matthew 3:10); and he cites the old husbandry books of Greece and the East, in which the owner of a barren tree approaches it with an axe and "an angry mind" as if to fell it, while another comes forward to deprecate the blow and make "himself responsible for its future fertility" — asking, "Does not our Lord refer to such a custom?"

Barnes maps the parable: God the owner; the Jewish people the vineyard; the coming for fruit, God's desire for good works; the barrenness, their wickedness; the dresser, the Saviour pleading; the waiting, the delay of vengeance "to give them opportunity of repentance." The witnesses thus see in the owner's word both justice and long patience. He had waited and waited, and a barren tree that only robbed the soil had no claim to stand. Yet the sentence "cut it down" is not the parable's last word — for it draws out the dresser's plea, and shows that even the order of judgment is given in the hearing of mercy (cf. Matthew 7:19).
8:
"And he answering said unto him, Lord, let it alone this year also, till I shall dig about it, and dung it:" The witnesses dwell on the dresser's intercession and the means of grace it describes.

The plea is one of intercession. Gill reads it of "the intercession of Christ" or "of the ministers of Christ and other good men; who, as Abraham interceded for Sodom, and Moses and Aaron for Israel, so do they for a sinful nation" (cf. Psalm 106:23). "And so the Jewish nation was spared for some time after this, though now deserving of immediate destruction." The "let it alone this year also" Barnes takes of "the delay of vengeance" (cf. 2 Peter 3:9; Romans 2:4).

"Dig about it, and dung it" the witnesses read as the appointed means of grace. Ambrose (on v. 6): "he promises that the hardness of their hearts shall be dug about by the Apostles' spades, lest a heap of earth cover up... the root of wisdom"; and the dung "is the grace of humility, by which even the fig is thought to become fruitful toward the Gospel." Theophylact: "to dig about it is to teach humility and patience, for the ground which has been dug is lowly." He even puts the plea into the mouth of Christ toward the Father: "Although through the Law and the Prophets they gave no fruit of repentance, I will water them with My sufferings and teaching, and perhaps they will yield us fruits of obedience"; and he hears in the dung the remembrance of sins, "for from the remembrance of sin the soul quickens itself to good works." Ellicott notes the homely image "suggests fertilising and gracious influences not less vividly than the dew or rain from heaven." Lightfoot brings the husbandman's actual practice from the Gloss: "they lay dung in their gardens to moisten the earth. They dig about the roots of their trees, they pluck up the suckers, they take off the leaves, they sprinkle ashes, and they smoke under the trees to kill worms" — no pains spared on a tree still hoped for. Robertson marks the homeliness of the word itself: κοπρία, "cast dung around it," a late word found here alone in the New Testament.

The witnesses thus see in the dresser's words the very heart of the gospel's patience. Where strict justice would fell the barren tree at once, mercy pleads for one more season and sets about every labour that might make it fruitful — digging, manuring, sparing no pains. So God, at the intercession of his Son and the prayers of his servants, delays the doom of the fruitless and brings to bear upon them every means of grace, that the tree which deserves the axe may yet, by humbling and quickening influences, be brought to bear.
9:
"And if it bear fruit, well: and if not, then after that thou shalt cut it down." The witnesses dwell on the conditional hope, the broken-off sentence, and the final judgment.

The words break off for effect. Robertson notes the "aposiopesis, sudden breaking off" — "if it bear fruit thenceforth," and then the unspoken "well" left to be supplied (cf. Matthew 3:10). Ellicott: "the sentence is elliptical, and the insertion of well... is needed to convey its meaning." He adds that some of the better manuscripts read "if it bear fruit in the time to come" — the year of grace stretching toward an undefined future. The very silence is eloquent — the longed-for outcome too good to need stating. Robertson recalls the story Trench tells of just such an intercession for a fig tree, for one year more, "widely current among the Arabs today who say that it will certainly bear fruit this time."

The fruit, if it come, is a blessing to all. Gill: "if hereby barren professors, as the Jews, become fruitful, it is well... it is well for themselves... for the churches where they are... and for the owner of the vineyard, and the dresser of it too."

But the alternative stands. "If not, then after that thou shalt cut it down." Bede (on v. 6) marks the fulfilment: this "came to pass under the Romans, by whom the Jewish nation was cut off, and thrust out from the land of promise." Theophylact carries the sentence beyond Rome to the last assize — "then after that you shall cut it down; namely, when you shall come to judge the quick and the dead. In the mean time it is now spared"; and Gregory presses the terror of that end: "he who will not by correction grow rich to fruitfulness, falls to that place from whence he is no more able to rise again by repentance." The principle is general — the barren tree, after every patience, is at last felled (cf. John 15:2; Hebrews 6:8). The witnesses thus see the parable close on the knife-edge between mercy and judgment. The dresser's plea wins a reprieve, not a reversal; the tree is spared, but only for a season and on condition. If the renewed labour of grace at last brings fruit, all is well; but if even that fails, the axe will fall, and justly. So the parable, spoken to a nation on the brink, holds out both the hope of one more year and the certainty that patience abused has an end — a warning, the commentators add, that reaches every soul that lives, fruitless, upon the long-suffering of God.
10:
"And he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the sabbath" — the opening of the healing of the bowed woman, which the voices read both historically and, with the fathers, as a figure of the synagogue and the soul set upright.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the setting. Robertson notes the periphrastic imperfect ἦν διδάσκων ("he was teaching," continued action); Ellicott observes the narrative is "peculiar to St. Luke," its very indefiniteness as to time and place suggesting one of the previously unrecorded traditions the evangelist gathered, confirmed by the use of "the Lord" in v.15. Gill adds the rabbinic detail that Jerusalem had hundreds of synagogues.

The fathers, gathered in the Catena, read the scene as the sequel to the fig-tree. Ambrose says the Lord "soon explained that He had been speaking of the synagogue," teaching in it on the Sabbath because the Jews were then hearing the law; the coming Sabbath is figured, when those who fulfil law and grace "shall by the mercy of God put off the toils of this weak body." Gregory and Augustine bind the woman to the fig-tree by number: "that which the three years signified in the tree, the eighteen did in the woman, for three times six is eighteen" — man made on the sixth day, bowed because before the law, under the law, and at the beginning of grace he was "unwilling to do perfect works." Gregory adds the searching note that Christ "sometimes calls but does not make upright," for habitual sin so binds the mind that, enlightened, it still cannot rise.

The Reformed Calvin frames the whole: the cure was performed on a woman "held by a spirit" of an extraordinary disease, displaying Christ's divine power triumphing over Satan — "not that Satan rules over men according to his pleasure, but only so far as God grants to him permission." The Puritan Henry draws the abiding lesson that "even bodily infirmities, unless very grievous, should not keep us from public worship on the sabbath days," since the woman, though long bowed and (it is supposed) in pain, came to the synagogue and there met her healer.
11:
"And, behold, there was a woman which had a spirit of infirmity eighteen years, and was bowed together, and could in no wise lift up herself" — the voices weigh the nature of the affliction and the agency of Satan behind it.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it with a physician's eye. Robertson, noting Luke's medical vocabulary, parses συνκύπτουσα ("bowed together," the "medical word for curvature of the spine") and the negative "could in no wise lift herself up… wholly" (εἰς τὸ παντελές, as in Hebrews 7:25) — "the poor old woman had to come in all bent over." Ellicott marks "the accuracy of the trained observer" in the duration, the symptoms of "permanent curvature of the spine," and the two participles; he reads "a spirit of infirmity" as an evil spirit producing bodily weakness, the seat of the powerlessness lying "in the region in which soul and body act and react," as in some forms of catalepsy. Barnes stresses that the long, real disease "shows that the miracle was real," and that "there in the sanctuary" the afflicted find consolation.

A quiet debate attends the cause. Adam Clarke, the wesleyan voice, asks the origin (sin), the agent (Satan), the nature (bent to the earth, "painful and humiliating"), and the duration ("a long time to be under the constant and peculiar influence of the devil"); Wesley wonders aloud, "Would not a modern physician have termed it a nervous case?" — registering, without endorsing, the natural diagnosis. The Reformed Calvin holds the two together: extraordinary diseases are "for the most part, inflicted on men through the agency of the devil," which gave "the more striking display of the divine power of Christ, which triumphed over Satan," though Satan acts only by God's permission. Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background — that "it was a most received opinion amongst the Jews, that diseases or plagues might be inflicted by the devil." Robertson adds that the genitive "of infirmity" is like "a spirit of bondage" (Romans 8:15), and Gill notes that, infirm as she was, she "got out to the place of worship," a rebuke to those who "upon every trifling indisposition, keep at home."
12:
"And when Jesus saw her, he called her to him, and said unto her, Woman, thou art loosed from thine infirmity" — the voices dwell on the unsought mercy and the authoritative word that healed.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark both the initiative and the grammar. Barnes notes the "remarkable declaration": "It does not appear that the woman applied to him for a cure; yet Jesus addressed her, and the disease departed," a clear proof that he was the Messiah and "mighty the power… that by a word could restore her." Robertson reads προσεφώνησεν ("he called her" to come to him) and the perfect ἀπολέλυσαι ("thou art loosed," loosed to stay free), "only N.T. example of use about disease." Ellicott hears in the words "a test of the woman's faith": would she, on hearing them, "make the effort to do what she had not done for eighteen years?" — the perfect tense showing "the work of healing was already completed."

The Reformed Calvin gathers the double proof the miracle gives "of his power and of his grace": Christ "authoritatively declares that deliverance was at his own disposal," testifying that "he had come for the purpose of granting relief to the wretched," and joining to the word the outward sign of his touch. Gill simply marks that he saw her "in the synagogue among the people." The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is that the cure was wholly of Christ's free initiative — she neither asked nor, so far as appears, hoped — and that his bare word, charged with divine authority, accomplished in an instant what eighteen years of infirmity had made hopeless; the preventing mercy of Christ, as Henry had said, met her before she called. It is this combination of sovereign initiative, authoritative word, and immediate, complete effect that the commentators take as the signature of his Messianic power and the pattern of his grace toward souls bowed down under spiritual bondage. The witnesses thus hold together three things they find equally remarkable — that Christ took the initiative toward one who neither asked nor hoped, that his word carried an authority proper to God alone, and that the cure was instant and entire — and in their union they read the surest token of his Messianic power and the very pattern of his preventing grace toward souls bowed under bondage.
13:
"And he laid his hands on her: and immediately she was made straight, and glorified God" — the brief comments dwell on the touch, the instant cure, and the praise it drew.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the words. Robertson parses ἐπέθηκεν ("he laid his hands upon her," as "the Great Physician with gentle kindness"), the effective aorist ἀνωρθώθη ("she was made straight again," the verb used only thrice in the New Testament, here "of making straight the old woman's crooked back"), and the inchoative imperfect ἐδόξαζεν — she "began it and kept it up," a continuous strain of praise. Ellicott reads the bodily act, as in the cures of the blind and dumb (Matthew 9:29), as "a help to the faith which was necessary, on the woman's part, that she might receive the full benefit," and notes that the tense of the verb implies she "poured forth her joy… in a continuous strain of praise." Barnes draws the plain duty: "they who are restored to health from sickness owe it to God; and they should devote their lives to his service."

The Reformed Calvin turns to the bystanders: the glorifying of God shows that this "was distinctly perceived to be a heavenly blessing," not "some doubtful work which allowed room for argument," but one affording "ample and undoubted grounds for praising God" — which only "discovers more strongly the malignity" of those about to cavil. Gill notes the laying on of hands as Christ's frequent manner in healing. The shared and grounded sense of the witnesses is the swift completeness of the cure, sealed by the woman's spontaneous praise: as Henry put it, when "crooked souls are made straight, they will show it by glorifying God," so the immediate, public, permanent straightening of her body becomes both the proof of Christ's almighty power and the type of the soul raised from its bondage to look up and bless its deliverer. The witnesses thus take the swift, public, permanent straightening of her body as both the proof of Christ's almighty power over a disease of eighteen years' standing and the type of the soul raised from its bondage to look up and bless its deliverer, the spontaneous praise being, as Henry said, the natural mark of a crooked soul made straight.
14:
"And the ruler of the synagogue answered with indignation, because that Jesus had healed on the sabbath day, and said unto the people, There are six days in which men ought to work" — the voices expose the ruler's hypocrisy and his abuse of the Sabbath law.

The grammatical-historical commentators dissect his conduct. Robertson notes that "no one had spoken to him," yet he "felt his importance as the ruler of the synagogue and was indignant" (ἀγανακτῶν, "to feel much pain"); his words "have a ludicrous sound," as if all the people had to do was "come round to his synagogue during the week" — forgetting that this woman "had been coming for eighteen years with no result" — and, angry with Jesus, he vents it on the multitude. Ellicott supplies the traditional law: the Jewish physician "might act in his calling in cases of emergency, life and death cases, but not in chronic diseases, such as this," the very check the ruler wished to impose. Barnes draws five lessons — men oppose good "because it is not done in their own way," look more at a supposed violation than at the good done, and act often "the result of envy" cloaked as "professed regard for religion."

The Reformed Calvin marks that the ruler "does not venture to pass censure openly on Christ, but points the venom of his dislike" at the multitude, indirectly condemning Christ in them — "an astonishing display of furious malice," defining the Sabbath so as to make God's grace "limited" and the holy assemblies pointless, "as if the power of God lay asleep" on the very day appointed for seeking his help. The Puritan Henry adds that the same Hebrew word means both "godly" and "merciful" (chesed), "to intimate that works of mercy and charity are in a manner works of piety" (1 Timothy 5:4) and therefore most proper on sabbath days — exposing how the ruler "stretches the law beyond its intention" to make a word's healing into forbidden work. The witnesses thus converge on a single charge: the ruler's "indignation," cloaked as zeal for the Sabbath, was in truth envy and enmity, and his attempt to send the people away "on the six days" only exposed how he had turned a day of mercy into a pretext for refusing it.
15:
"The Lord then answered him, and said, Thou hypocrite, doth not each one of you on the sabbath loose his ox or his ass from the stall, and lead him away to watering?" — the voices admire the crushing argument from the Jews' own practice.

The grammatical-historical commentators relish its force. Robertson notes the renewed use of "the Lord" in Luke's narrative and calls it "a crushing and overwhelming reply": these very critics "cared too much for an ox or an ass to leave it all the sabbath without water," so the plural "hypocrites" fits "all who agree with him." Barnes states the retort plainly: "You condemn me for doing to a woman what you do to a beast." Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic rules that permitted leading cattle out to watering on the Sabbath, even drawing and pouring water into troughs "provided only that they do not carry the water" — grounding Christ's argument in the actual halakhah his opponents observed.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the twofold comparison: "that of the cattle with the" woman, "and that of the halter… with the chains of Satan, by which he holds men bound to their destruction." If it is lawful "to perform the offices of humanity to cattle, it is ridiculous to imagine" the Sabbath forbids assistance to the children of God, "especially when the necessity is more urgent" — for one is to be "delivered from" Satan. He observes, too, that Christ "never performed any work, however illustrious, which wicked men did not seize as an occasion for slander." The wesleyan Wesley simply pierces the motive: "Thou hypocrite — for the real motive of his speaking was envy, not (as he pretended) pure zeal for the glory of God." Across the witnesses the shared and grounded note is that Christ exposes the ruler out of his own observed custom, convicting him of valuing a thirsty beast above a suffering daughter of Abraham. Robertson adds that the watering of cattle was so universal a practice that the critics "cared too much for an ox or an ass to leave it all the sabbath without water," so that their own daily mercy to beasts became the unanswerable measure of their cruelty to a suffering daughter of Abraham.
16:
"And ought not this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan hath bound, lo, these eighteen years, be loosed from this bond on the sabbath day?" — the voices unfold the threefold argument and the open naming of Satan as the binder.

The grammatical-historical commentators count the steps. Robertson lays out the "triple argument": she is "a human being and not an ox or ass," a woman, and "a daughter of Abraham (Jewess), besides being old and ill"; the imperfect οὐκ ἔδει means "of necessity… Jesus simply had to heal her even if on the sabbath," and ἣν ἔδησεν ὁ Σατανᾶς is a "definite statement that her disease was due to Satan." Barnes explains "daughter of Abraham" as her Jewish descent, which should have made the ruler "rejoice that she was loosed," and is careful that "it was not properly a possession of the devil… but God had suffered him to afflict her," as with Job (Job 1:12). Ellicott reads "whom Satan hath bound" as implying that "all disease — or very many forms of it — is directly or indirectly traceable to the power of the Enemy," comparing Paul's "messenger of Satan" (2 Corinthians 12:7), and movingly imagines "the beloved physician" Luke practising his art on the Sabbath and finding in these words a sanction for his own work.

The Reformed Gill deepens the title: she was "in a spiritual sense, a daughter of Abraham, that walked in the steps of his faith," now "a believer in Christ." The wesleyan Wesley presses the a fortiori — "ought not any human creature, which is so far better than an ox or an ass? Much more, this daughter of Abraham… to be loosed?" The shared and grounded sense is the irresistible necessity ("ought not") of mercy: a believing daughter of Abraham, bound eighteen years by Satan himself, has a claim on the Sabbath far above the beast the ruler unhesitatingly waters. The witnesses thus read the verse as the climax of the controversy: an irresistible "ought not" of compassion, in which the believing daughter of Abraham, bound eighteen years by Satan himself, has a claim upon the Sabbath rest far surpassing that of the beast the ruler unhesitatingly looses and leads to water.
17:
"And when he had said these things, all his adversaries were ashamed: and all the people rejoiced for all the glorious things that were done by him" — the voices mark the sharp contrast between the silenced foes and the gladdened crowd.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the opposed emotions and their social shape. Robertson reads the imperfect passive κατῃσχύνοντο ("were put to shame," made "to blush with shame at their predicament") against the imperfect active ἔχαιρεν ("rejoiced"), "a sharp contrast in the emotions of the two groups," with γινομένοις (present participle) for the things "continually being done." Barnes draws the recurring pattern: "those who opposed Christ were chiefly the rulers," who "had an interest" and feared he would "draw off the people," while "the common people heard him gladly" — "the condition of the poor… is by far the most favourable for religion."

The Puritan Henry weighs the two responses: the adversaries "were put to silence, and were vexed," a shame that "worked not repentance, but rather indignation," yet a token that "sooner or later, all the adversaries of Christ… will be made ashamed" (1 Peter 3:16); the people's joy was "the shame of his foes," for "every thing that is the honour of Christ is the comfort of Christians." The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem, drawn into the Catena on the theme of the Spirit's gifts rather than the verse, dwells on Christ breathing on the apostles (John 20:22) — an associative excerpt characteristic of how the catechetical fragments attach to a text. The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is the double effect of Christ's vindicated mercy: it covered his enemies with confusion and filled his friends with rejoicing, the glory of his works overcoming "every scruple in them who sought Him not with corrupt hearts." The witnesses thus read the verse as the controversy's resolution: Christ's vindicated mercy at once covered his adversaries with a confusion that bred indignation rather than repentance and filled the common people with a joy that was the very honour of Christ and, as Henry says, the comfort of his followers.
18:
"Then said he, Unto what is the kingdom of God like? and whereunto shall I resemble it? It is like a grain of mustard seed" — the voices, while referring the parable's exposition to its fuller place (Matthew 13:31), here gather the fathers' rich allegory of the seed and the leaven.

The fathers read the mustard seed Christologically and ecclesially. The Gloss and Ambrose make faith itself "the kingdom of heaven which is within us," small as a seed but pouring forth power when crushed — "the martyrs are grains of mustard seed" whose bruising scatters "the seeds of their martyrdom" to the world's ends; and "the Lord Himself also is a grain of mustard seed," sown and buried in a garden, rising "a tree overshadowing the world," in whose branches Peter and Paul are boughs. Cyril makes the kingdom the Gospel, at first "in the possession only of few" but afterward spread abroad; Bede the preaching that "grows… in the mind of every believer, for no one is suddenly made perfect." On the leaven, Ambrose, Augustine, and Bede offer a cluster of readings — Christ the leaven excelling "in strength, not in appearance"; the three measures the race of mankind from Noah's sons, or the Father, Son, and Spirit, or the whole man of body, soul, and spirit; Bede taking the leaven as "love… hid in three measures" because we are bidden love God with heart, mind, and strength.

The grammatical-historical commentators are content to defer. Barnes, Clarke, and Wesley each refer the parables to their exposition in Matthew 13:31. Robertson observes the parable, "given in the first great group of parables" in Mark and Matthew, is "just the sort to be repeated," and confesses it "is not clear to what to refer 'therefore'" — whether to the woman of v.11, the crowd's enthusiasm of v.17, or something Luke did not record. The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is the same the parable everywhere teaches: the kingdom's seemingly negligible beginnings — a seed, a hidden pinch of leaven — issue in vast, pervasive, sheltering growth, a truth the fathers expand devotionally and the modern expositors fix exegetically.
19:
"It is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and cast into his garden; and it grew, and waxed a great tree; and the fowls of the air lodged in the branches of it" — the voices treat the seed's smallness and its great growth, deferring the full exposition to the Synoptic parallels.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the realia and the diction. Robertson notes the Jewish proverb "small as a mustard seed," that the plant could "grow to twelve feet at times," and Luke's distinctive details — "cast into his own garden" (κῆπος, "garden," against Mark's "earth" and Matthew's "field") and "became a tree" (ἐγένετο εἰς δένδρον), "a common Hebraism, very frequent in the Septuagint," with κατεσκήνωσεν for the birds' lodging. Barnes simply refers the parable to its explanation in Matthew 13:31, and Gill takes the smallness and after-increase of the seed for "the ministry of the Gospel… the Gospel church state, and… the grace of God in the hearts of his people."

The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem supplies the devotional register the modern voices omit, drawing on the Lord's word "if ye have faith as a grain of mustard seed" (Matthew 17:20): "just as the grain of mustard seed is small in size, but fiery in its operation, and… when grown up is able even to shelter the fowls; so likewise, faith in the swiftest moment works the greatest effects in the soul." For when enlightened by faith, he says, the soul "hath visions of God… and before the end of this world already beholds the Judgment." The shared and grounded sense across the witnesses is the parable's single point: a beginning so small as to be proverbial for insignificance grows, by the kingdom's hidden vitality, into a great tree wide enough to shelter the birds — whether read of the Gospel's spread through the world, the church's growth, or faith's swift and "fiery" working in the believing soul. Robertson adds that the lodging of the birds (κατεσκήνωσεν, to make nest) pictures the shelter the grown tree affords, so that the parable's single point stands clear across every reading — Gospel, church, or believing soul — the negligible seed becoming a great tree wide enough to shelter the fowls of the air.
20:
"And again he said, Whereunto shall I liken the kingdom of God?" — the repeated question introducing the parable of the leaven, which the witnesses treat briefly, sending the reader to its fuller form in the Synoptics.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the doubled question and the parallel. Robertson observes that the question "Whereunto shall I liken?" stands "alone in Luke here as in v.18," but that "the parable is precisely like that in Matthew 13:33," to which he refers for details. Barnes likewise sends the reader to the explanation of the kingdom-parables in Matthew 13:33, and Wesley simply cites the parallel. Gill takes the renewed saying as introducing, "besides the parable of the grain of mustard seed, that also of the leaven hid in three measures of meal."

The deliberate doubling — seed and leaven, the one a picture of outward, visible growth, the other of inward, secret permeation — is itself the witnesses' grounded observation: Luke preserves the pair, as Matthew does, so that the kingdom is shown spreading both outward like a tree that overtops the garden and inward like leaven working unseen through the whole lump. The patristic allegories gathered on v.18 — Christ or love or the Spirit as the leaven, the three measures as mankind or the triune God or the whole man of body, soul, and spirit — belong here too, and the fathers freely carry them across the two verses. The shared and grounded sense the commentators draw, even in their brevity, is that the kingdom of God, however hidden and however small its first working, will not rest until, like leaven, it has leavened the whole — a quiet, irresistible, transforming power at work in the world and in the soul, whose end is a complete and pervasive renewal. Robertson is careful that the parable "is precisely like" Matthew's, so the witnesses add little beyond fixing the pair; yet the very preservation of seed and leaven together is their grounded observation, the kingdom shown spreading at once outwardly like an overtopping tree and inwardly like leaven working unseen until the whole lump is changed.
21:
"It is like leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened" — the voices read the leaven's small quantity and pervasive, spreading power, dividing over whether it pictures the Gospel's good working or a creeping corruption.

The chief debate belongs to the Reformed Gill, who allows both senses: the leaven "fitly expresses the small beginnings of the Gospel ministry, and its increase, also the state… of Gospel churches, and the nature of the grace of God" — "unless false doctrine should rather be meant, which privately, secretly, and by little and little, got into the churches of Christ… as in the times of the papacy." The leaven's hiddenness can thus figure either the saving truth quietly transforming the whole or the error secretly corrupting it. The wesleyan Wesley keeps to the simpler note of concealment: "covered up — so that, for a time, nothing of it appeared."

The grammatical-historical commentators again defer to the parallel. Barnes and Clarke both refer the leaven "explained" to Matthew 13:33, where "three measures of meal" is noted, content to let the exposition stand there. The patristic readings gathered on v.18 — Bede taking the leaven for love hid in three measures because we must love God with heart, mind, and strength; Ambrose and Augustine for Christ or the Spirit, the three measures for mankind from Noah's sons or for the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost — supply the devotional fullness the modern voices omit. The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is the parable's plain force: a small thing, wholly hidden in the mass, works silently and completely until "the whole was leavened" — a picture, on the dominant reading, of the kingdom's quiet, total, transforming power in the world and in the heart, and, on Gill's minority caution, a reminder that the same image of secret spreading can warn against the unseen working of false teaching. Robertson and the modern voices, content to defer, thus leave the devotional fullness to the fathers, but the witnesses agree on the parable's plain force: a small thing wholly hidden in the mass works silently and completely "till the whole was leavened," a picture of the kingdom's quiet, total, transforming power in the world and in the heart.
22:
"And he went through the cities and villages, teaching, and journeying toward Jerusalem. Then said one unto him, Lord, are there few that be saved?" — the voices treat the journey, the question, and Christ's redirecting answer.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the itinerary. Ellicott and Robertson read this as the continuation of the journey begun at Luke 9:51 — "making a journey" implying "a circuit deliberately planned" — Ellicott locating it chiefly in Peraea east of the Jordan, with the retirements implied in Matthew 19:1 and John 10:40. Robertson calls it "the second of the journeys to Jerusalem in this later ministry," Barnes drawing the diligence: "though on a journey," Christ "did not excuse himself on the plea that he was in haste." Calvin is uncertain whether one journey or Christ's settled custom of attending the festivals is meant.

The fathers turn the question to its profit. The Gloss connects "are there few that be saved?" with the foregoing parable, where the birds lodging in the branches might suggest "many"; but Gregory observes that "it was our Saviour's custom to answer those who asked Him, not according as they might judge right… but with regard to what might be profitable," and so, rather than say whether few or many, he turns to "the way by which man may come to salvation" — "Strive to enter in at the strait gate." Basil pictures the broad ease of error against the narrow bridge of the right way; Cyril makes the narrow gate "the toils and sufferings of the saints," and Chrysostom reconciles it with "my yoke is easy," since "whatever is troublesome to our nature may be considered easy when we undertake it heartily." Augustine harmonizes "few" with "many shall come from the east": few "in comparison with those who are lost, many when united with the angels." The shared and grounded note is that Christ refuses the speculative question and presses each hearer to strive for his own entrance. The witnesses thus read the exchange as a model of Christ's method: declining the idle, speculative tally of the saved, he turns the questioner — and through him the crowd — to the one needful and profitable thing, the strenuous personal pursuit of entrance at the strait gate.
23:
"Then said one unto him, Lord, are there few that be saved? And he said unto them, Strive to enter in at the strait gate" — the voices probe the questioner's motive and the present tense of "saved."

The grammatical-historical commentators read the question as idle speculation. Ellicott insists the Greek participle is present — "are being saved," or "in the way of salvation" — and the same care is needed at 2 Corinthians 2:15 and Acts 2:47 (where the AV's "such as should be saved" is "a singular infelicity"); he sets the question against "the fevered, anxious thoughts" of the age, citing 2 Esdras' "many are created, but few shall be saved," and concludes that "a man anxious and in earnest would have asked, 'What must I do to be saved?'" — so Christ "put it aside, gave no direct answer," and forced the man back on his own striving. Barnes agrees it was "a question of idle curiosity"; Robertson calls it "an academic theological problem with the rabbis, the number of the elect." Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic disputes over how many of Israel should have a part in the world to come.

The wesleyan Clarke turns it sharply practical: "a question either of impertinence or curiosity, the answer to which can profit no man. The grand question is, Can I be saved? Yes. How? Strive earnestly… ἀγωνίζεσθε, agonize — exert every power of body and soul." The Reformed Calvin sets the question in its likely occasion — that Christ, "who declared himself to be the author of life, could with difficulty collect a small number of disciples" — and reads the answer as withdrawing his people "from a foolish curiosity… as if they were unwilling to be saved but in a crowd," while teaching "that it is impossible to obtain eternal life without great and appalling difficulties." The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is that Christ deliberately declines the speculative tally and presses the individual to a strenuous, personal pursuit of salvation. The witnesses thus agree that Christ deliberately refuses the speculative tally and presses the individual to a strenuous, personal pursuit of salvation, reading in the very form of the question ("are there few?" rather than "what must I do?") the mark of a curiosity that the answer was framed to rebuke and redirect.
24:
"Strive to enter in at the strait gate: for many, I say unto you, will seek to enter in, and shall not be able" — the voices press the agonizing effort required and the tragedy of those who merely "seek."

The grammatical-historical commentators feel the athletic force of the verb. Barnes: "Literally, agonize. The word is taken from the Grecian Games," where competitors "put forth all their powers to gain the victory" (so 1 Corinthians 9:24; Philippians 2:16; Hebrews 12:1) — we must "make it our first and chief business" to enter. Robertson notes the present middle ἀγωνίζεσθε ("our agonize"), kin to the ἀγωνία of Gethsemane (Luke 22:44), and that here the strait gate is "the entrance to the house," the "narrow door" (θύρας), not the outside gate of Matthew 7:13. Ellicott marks the variation from Matthew's "enter ye in" to "strive to enter," the same word as in 1 Timothy 6:12. On "shall not be able," Barnes draws the solemn point: many "seek it, but do not agonize for it," and the more probable sense refers their seeking "to a time… too late" — when "the master has risen up" and the door is shut.

The wesleyan Clarke and Wesley sharpen the contrast: many "seek — wish and desire; but they do not strive," and "because they will not agonize — will not be in earnest, they shall not get in"; "barely seeking will not avail." The Reformed Calvin reads the warning against "a vain hope, as if the multitude of our companions would be of any avail": those who "calculate that their possession of life is already certain" will be "shut out," for many "who now give themselves every indulgence, promise to themselves an easy entrance into life." The shared and grounded note is that entrance is by strenuous, present, personal effort, and that a mere wish, deferred till the door is shut, will find itself "not able." The witnesses thus converge on a single, urgent demand: entrance is by strenuous, present, personal effort — to agonize as the wrestler agonizes — and a mere wish, deferred until the master has risen and the door is shut, will find itself, however loud its later knocking, simply "not able" to enter.
25:
"When once the master of the house is risen up, and hath shut to the door… ye begin to stand without, and to knock at the door, saying, Lord, Lord, open unto us; and he shall answer… I know you not whence ye are" — the voices read the shut door as the close of the day of grace.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the figure from the householder. Barnes: there is "a proper time to seek an entrance into heaven; but there will be a time when it will be too late. At death the time will have passed by." Robertson weighs whether this is a second parable (verses 25–30, "about not being too late") distinct from verse 24 ("about being in earnest"), parses the difficult ἀφ' οὗ ἄν construction, and renders ἀποκλείσῃ "slammed the door fast" (perfective ἀπο-), with the urgent first-aorist imperative "open to us." Ellicott notes the passage blends elements common to Matthew 7:22 and Matthew 25:10.

The Reformed Calvin makes the householder one who, learning that "wicked and dissolute persons" steal out by night and expose the house to thieves, "does not allow those night-prowlers to enter": so men must "avail themselves of the opportunity, while it is offered," for "so long as the Lord invites us to himself, the" door "is, as it were, open," but "the greater part do not deign to move a step," and Christ threatens that it will "at length be" shut. The wesleyan Wesley presses the terror of the moment: "even agonizing will not avail, after the door is shut. Agonize, therefore, now by faith, prayer, holiness, patience"; and on "I know you not," "I approve not of your ways." Gill reads the master "risen up" as the Gospel feast ended, the espoused brought in, "long patience and forbearance" expired. The shared and grounded note is the irrevocable shutting of the door: a bounded day of grace, after which the latest, loudest knocking meets only the dreadful "I know you not whence ye are." The witnesses thus read the shut door as the irrevocable close of the day of grace: a bounded season of invitation, seized by few, after which the latest and loudest knocking meets only the dreadful "I know you not whence ye are," and even agonizing, as Wesley warns, will then no longer avail.
26:
"Then shall ye begin to say, We have eaten and drunk in thy presence, and thou hast taught in our streets" — the voices expose the worthlessness of a merely external acquaintance with Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the plea as false confidence. Barnes (comparing Matthew 7:22) takes eating with one as "evidence of acquaintanceship or friendship," the excluded alleging "that they had professed attachment" — but it shows "on how slight evidence men will suppose themselves ready to die," resting on "a profession of religion, attendance on the preaching of the word or at the sacraments, or a decent external life," none of which "will avail in the day of judgment"; only "true love to God, a real change of heart, and a life of piety" can save. He is careful that "there is no allusion here… to the sacrament." Ellicott notes the plea is humbler than Matthew's "did we not prophesy?": these are not office-holders but men who "had once sat at meat… with the Prophet whom they acknowledged as the Christ," and, by extension, all who "eat and drink with Him now in visible fellowship with His Church" yet rest their hope "on that outward communion." Robertson finds it "grotesque to claim credit because Christ taught in their streets," but "they are hard run for excuses."

The Reformed Calvin states it plainly: "it will be of no advantage to the Jews, that he approached near to them, and permitted them to enjoy familiar intercourse with him, if, when called, they do not answer at the appointed day"; here Christ drops the figure and "states… that he is himself the judge." The fathers gathered above (Theophylact, Bede) add that Christians too "eat the body of Christ and drink His blood" and hear him teach "in the streets of our souls," yet "the knowledge of the Scriptures does not make him known to God, whom the iniquity of his works proves… unworthy." The shared and grounded note is that nearness to Christ — table-fellowship, hearing his teaching, sacramental communion — counts for nothing without a transformed heart and a holy life.
27:
"But he shall say, I tell you, I know you not whence ye are; depart from me, all ye workers of iniquity" — the voices hear the final, decisive sentence that sweeps away every false plea.

The grammatical-historical Robertson marks how "this blunt statement cuts the matter short and sweeps away the flimsy cobwebs": "acquaintance with Christ in the flesh does not open the door." He notes that Jesus here echoes the Psalm ("depart from me, all ye workers of iniquity," cf. Psalm 6:8) as in Matthew 7:23, but with ἀπόστητε ("depart," second aorist imperative) where Matthew has ἀποχωρεῖτε. Barnes simply refers the verse to the parallel sentence of rejection (Matthew 7:22; Matthew 25:41), and Gill, with the Persic version, hears the indignation — "be gone from my sight, and be far from my door" — as the Master's abhorrence of those he "cannot bear… in his sight."

The decisive words are twofold, and the witnesses weigh both. The first, "I know you not whence ye are," means (as Gregory said on the surrounding verses) not ignorance but rejection — "for God not to know is for Him to reject." The second, "depart from me, ye workers of iniquity," supplies the ground of the rejection: not the absence of profession but the presence of unrighteousness, so that, as the fathers urged, the knowledge of the Scriptures and a place at the table avail nothing where "the iniquity of his works proves" a man unworthy. The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is the terrible finality of the verdict: those who had pleaded their nearness to Christ are disowned, not because they lacked acquaintance, but because their lives were lawless — the door shut, the plea refused, and the sentence "depart" pronounced upon all whose works belied their words. The witnesses thus weigh both halves of the sentence — "I know you not" as rejection rather than ignorance, and "depart… ye workers of iniquity" as the ground of it — concluding that nearness to Christ and knowledge of the Scriptures avail nothing where the life is lawless, and the door, once shut, is shut for ever.
28:
"There shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when ye shall see Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, and all the prophets, in the kingdom of God, and you yourselves thrust out" — the voices weigh the sorrow of the excluded and the entry of the patriarchs.

The grammatical-historical commentators refer the saying to its companion in Matthew 8:11. Robertson reads the emphatic "there" (ἐκεῖ, "out there, outside the house whence they are driven"), the aorist subjunctive "when ye shall see," and the present passive "you being cast out" — "continuous action… with the door shut." Barnes and Clarke send the reader to the parallel for the figure of weeping and gnashing, Bede (gathered above) reading the two as "the feeling cold and heat" of hell, or the gnashing as the late, self-directed anger of one "who repents too late."

The Reformed Calvin presses the sting for the Jews: they "bore no resemblance to the holy fathers, and had no right to boast of being descended from them," yet "nothing was more customary than to abuse the title of the Church"; Christ assures them "that a bastard race, which has departed from the faith and piety of the fathers, has no inheritance" (Ephesians 5:5), with "a silent but implied reproof" that those who sought companions in salvation did not seek to join Abraham and the holy fathers but looked round among their degenerate contemporaries. The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem, drawn in on the fruits of repentance, urges the sharing of coat and meat and holds out hope even to the publican and harlot who repent, "for sin committed in… ignorance is pardoned, but persistent wickedness is condemned." The shared and grounded note is the bitter reversal: the natural heirs, seeing the patriarchs feasting within and themselves shut out, are filled with unavailing grief, their physical descent from Abraham counting for nothing against their departure from his faith. The witnesses thus read the verse as a bitter reversal: the natural heirs, seeing the patriarchs and prophets feasting within and themselves thrust out, are filled with unavailing grief, their physical descent from Abraham counting for nothing against their departure from his faith, while even publicans and harlots who truly repent may enter before them.
29:
"And they shall come from the east, and from the west, and from the north, and from the south, and shall sit down in the kingdom of God" — the voices read the gathering of the Gentiles into the patriarchs' feast.

The grammatical-historical commentators keep to the picture. Robertson reads ἀνακλιθήσονται ("shall sit down," the figure of "recline… of this heavenly banquet") and is careful that "Jesus does not mean that these will be saved in different ways, but only that many will come from all the four quarters of the earth." Barnes and Clarke refer the verse to its parallel (Matthew 8:11), Clarke drawing the plain sense: "the Gentiles, in every part of the world, shall receive the Gospel of the grace of God, when the Jews shall have rejected it." Gill notes the aggravation to the Jews — these incoming Gentiles "will be a greater aggravation of the punishment" and "cause still more rage and madness."

The Reformed Calvin draws the larger design: the Jews, "who reckoned themselves the only lawful heirs of God," are to be rejected, "and the Gentiles… substituted in their room," obtaining "the life which was promised to Abraham and his posterity"; Christ contrasts the Gentiles with them "to excite them to faith by a holy jealousy," as Paul does (Romans 11:13). The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is the universal ingathering: from every quarter of the earth the once-distant nations are brought to recline at the one table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — a gracious widening of the kingdom that is, at the same time, a stinging rebuke to the natural heirs who, trusting their lineage, find strangers in their place. The Catena's Eusebius adds that the fathers, before the law, "forsaking the sins of many gods," received the knowledge of the most high God, to whom "many of the Gentiles were conformed through a similar manner of life," while their own children "suffered estrangement." The witnesses thus read the verse as the gracious widening of the kingdom and, at the same time, a stinging rebuke to the natural heirs: from every quarter of the earth the once-distant nations are brought to recline at the one table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, while those who trusted their lineage find strangers in their place.
30:
"And, behold, there are last which shall be first, and there are first which shall be last" — the voices read the great reversal, here applied first to Jew and Gentile.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the saying's currency and its primary reference. Robertson observes it "was repeated many times" (Matthew 19:30; Mark 10:31; Matthew 20:16). Ellicott marks that "in point of time… this is the first utterance of the great law that God's judgment reverses man's"; here "the application is primarily national" — Israel, "the first of nations," should "become, in its outward fortunes, the last," while the heathen, once "without hope and without God in the world" (Ephesians 2:12), gain "the high pre-eminence of being the heirs of the kingdom" — though "the individual application… grows naturally" out of the national. Gill makes the "last" the Gentiles, "most mean and abject… called last of all," who "shall be first, and sit down among patriarchs and prophets."

The Reformed Calvin keeps the verse to its purpose here: though the same words are "frequently employed by Christ… in a different sense" (Matthew 19:30; Matthew 20:16), "all that he intended here was, to throw down the vain confidence of the Jews," who, "chosen by God in preference to all the rest of the world, trusted to this distinction," so that their "condition will soon be changed" — the Gentiles obtaining "the first rank," the Jews not even occupying "the farthest corner in the Church." The wesleyan Wesley applies it more individually — "many of the Gentiles who were latest called, shall be most highly rewarded; and many of the Jews who were first called, shall have no reward at all." The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is the overturning of human precedence: privilege and priority of calling are no guarantee, and the despised and last-come may, by faith, take the foremost place in the kingdom of God. The witnesses thus read the verse as the overturning of human precedence: privilege and priority of calling are no guarantee of place, and the despised and last-come may, by faith, take the foremost room in the kingdom of God, while the first, presuming on their standing, are set last.
31:
"The same day there came certain of the Pharisees, saying unto him, Get thee out, and depart hence: for Herod will kill thee" — the voices debate whether the warning was friendly, a Pharisaic ruse, or sent by Herod himself.

The chief debate is over the Pharisees' motive. Robertson sees "the Pharisees in a new role, warning Jesus against… Herod, when they are plotting themselves." Ellicott canvasses three views — that they "reported what they actually knew, out of feelings more or less friendly," or "invented a false tale" to be rid of him, or "were sent by Herod" with a threat — and judges from Christ's reply ("Go tell that fox") that "the last of these views" is "the most probable," connecting it with the Peraean ministry near Machaerus, where Herod had a palace, and tracing perhaps "the hand of Herodias," reminiscent of Jezebel's message to Elijah (1 Kings 19:2). Barnes thinks it "more than possible that Herod might have sent these Pharisees," wishing Jesus removed but not daring openly to kill him. Wesley allows the gentler reading — "possibly they gave him the caution out of good will."

The fathers read it spiritually. Cyril says the Pharisees, fearing the loss of "their office as rulers" and "their gains," "with pretended love for Him… persuade Him to depart." Bede sees in the "fox" a type of heretics, "full of craft… never walking in straight paths," who seek "to destroy Christ… in the hearts of believers." The Reformed Calvin is "strongly inclined to conjecture" that the Pharisees "attempted to drive him to some other place, because they saw that the greater part of the people… were attached to Christ," their true design "to awaken in him such fears as would drive him to some place of concealment" — and behind them "the first originator and contriver of this scheme, Satan." The shared and grounded note is that, whatever the human motive, Christ saw through it and would not be frightened from his course. The witnesses thus agree that, whatever the human motive behind the warning — friendly caution, Pharisaic ruse, or Herod's veiled threat — Christ saw through it entirely and would not be frightened from his appointed course, answering the craft of fox and Pharisee alike with serene independence.
32:
"And he said unto them, Go ye, and tell that fox, Behold, I cast out devils, and I do cures to day and to morrow, and the third day I shall be perfected" — the voices weigh the epithet "fox," the "three days," and "perfected."

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on "fox." Barnes takes it as "an emblem of slyness, of cunning, and of artful mischief," the point being not Herod's vices but his "art… in endeavouring to remove him… by stratagem"; Robertson reads "the cunning and cowardice of Herod," the message meaning "he is independent of the plots… of both Herod and the Pharisees." Ellicott notes the Greek word for fox "is always used as a feminine," giving "a special touch of indignant force" — Herod "had so identified himself with Herodias that he had lost his manliness." On "to day and to morrow… the third day," most read "a short, undefined interval" in proverbial speech (so Ellicott, Barnes, comparing Exodus 5:14), while Clarke and Wesley incline to a more literal reckoning — "on the third day I shall be put to death." On τελειοῦμαι ("I shall be perfected"), Barnes glosses "I shall have ended my course here"; Clarke and Robertson connect it with the verb's use of Christ's consummation in suffering (Hebrews 2:10; Hebrews 5:9), "perfect humanity" being "a process" Christ was passing through "without sin, but not without… suffering."

A debate attends the epithet's target. Adam Clarke, citing Bishop Pearce, doubts whether Herod — who "never seemed studious to conceal his vices" — is meant, suggesting the "fox" may be "that man… from whom the advice… came" (the Pharisee messenger); Lightfoot, with the proverb "worship the fox in his time," reads Christ's defiance — "however he may wallow in his present prosperity… I will never flatter him." The Reformed Calvin and the wesleyan Wesley refer it to Herod's craft and cowardice, Wesley adding the caution that Christ's "extraordinary office justified him in using that severity of language" toward wicked princes "to which we have no call." The shared and grounded note is Christ's serene independence: undeterred by the threat, he will finish his appointed work of mercy and then, at his own hour, be perfected.
33:
"Nevertheless I must walk to day, and to morrow, and the day following: for it cannot be that a prophet perish out of Jerusalem" — the voices read Christ's fixed purpose and the bitter irony against Jerusalem.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the resolve and the irony. Ellicott renders "I must journey… I must go onward" (the word of Luke 9:51), conveying "the intensity of conviction and of purpose," and reads "it cannot be" as "a peculiar half-ironical force — it would be at variance with the fitness of things… morally impossible," Jerusalem having "made the slaughter of the prophets a special prerogative, a monopoly." Robertson agrees it is "a severely ironical indictment of Jerusalem," and notes "the shadow of the Cross reaches Perea." Barnes gives the plain sense: "I have no fear that Herod will put me to death in Galilee… Jerusalem is the place where the prophets die, and where I am to die," his life "safe until I arrive at Jerusalem." Lightfoot and Barnes add the legal ground — a prophet, tried as a false prophet, could be judged only by the great Sanhedrin at Jerusalem.

A small debate attends "to day and to morrow." Clarke, citing Kypke, proposes "I must walk to-day and to-morrow in the neighbouring coasts," placing Christ in Peraea; Ellicott refuses any literal three-day count — "it would not have been true in fact" — reading the same proverbial formula as in the previous verse. The Reformed Calvin presses the second clause as a sharp attack on the Pharisees: "Is it you, who… will be my executioners, that advise me to beware of" Herod? Jerusalem had long been "a den of robbers, in which almost all the" prophets "had been murdered" — the very city "chosen to be the sanctuary of divine worship" polluted "by a regular butchery of the" prophets. The shared and grounded note is Christ's unshaken advance: no threat can hasten or delay his death, which is fixed by God for Jerusalem, the prophet-slaying city. The witnesses thus read the verse as Christ's unshaken advance toward his death: no threat of Herod can either hasten or delay it, for it is fixed by God for Jerusalem, the prophet-slaying city, whose grim monopoly on the blood of the prophets makes it, by a terrible fitness, the only place where the Lord of the prophets must perish.
34:
"O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, which killest the prophets… how often would I have gathered thy children together, as a hen doth gather her brood under her wings, and ye would not!" — the voices weigh the lament, the repeated name, and the maternal image.

The chief debate is whether Christ spoke this lament once or twice. Robertson notes the nearly identical apostrophe in Matthew 23:37 and, against Plummer's "rather violent hypothesis," sees no reason "why Jesus could not have made the lament both here and in Jerusalem," the repeated visits implied in "how often" agreeing with John's record. Gill holds plainly that "the same words were spoken by Christ at different times, in different places, and to different persons," though he allows Luke may have inserted them here on the mention of a prophet perishing in Jerusalem. Robertson supplies the philology — ἐπισυνάξαι (Luke) against Matthew's ἐπισυναγαγεῖν, νοσσιάν ("brood") against νοσσία ("chickens") — and the "how often" (ποσάκις) "clearly showing that Jesus made repeated visits to Jerusalem."

The fathers read the figure tenderly. Bede notes that in calling on Jerusalem Christ "addresses not the stones… but the dwellers therein," weeping "with the affection of a father"; the twice-repeated name betokens, says Chrysostom, "compassion or very great love," as "a lover" to a mistress who despised him. Cyril makes the gathering the long history of grace — "He led them by the hand of Moses… He warns them by His prophets… He wished to have them under His wings" — but "they deprived themselves… through their ingratitude," Augustine adding that the gathering "was done by my all-prevailing will, yet your unwillingness." The Catena observes the apt contrast: he who had called Herod a "fox" now "compares Himself to a bird, for foxes are ever lying in wait for birds." The wesleyan Wesley counts "three solemn visits" since the baptism "for this very purpose," and Cyril of Jerusalem uses the "wings" to guard against crude images of God, the protecting power "conceived as wings." The shared and grounded note is the grief of a rejected love: a Saviour who longed, again and again, to shelter his people, met by a settled unwillingness.
35:
"Behold, your house is left unto you desolate: and verily I say unto you, Ye shall not see me, until the time come when ye shall say, Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord" — the voices weigh "your house" and the puzzling "ye shall not see me."

The grammatical-historical commentators expound "house" and the promise. Clarke takes ὁ οἶκος as "the temple — called here your house, not my house — I acknowledge it no longer; I have abandoned it," comparing the LORD's "thy people" to Moses (Exodus 32:7), though some read it of "the whole commonwealth of the Jews." Barnes refers "your house is left… desolate" to the prophetic denunciations (Leviticus 26:31–32; Micah 3:12) and "Blessed is he that cometh" to the triumphal entry (Luke 19:38; John 12:13). Lightfoot confesses the difficulty: "my old eyes see better than my younger ones did," puzzling how "ye shall not see me" fits, when after these words "they saw him conversant amongst them" for months, even at the triumphal acclamation — whereas in Matthew the words follow that triumph.

A debate attends the meaning of "until ye shall say, Blessed is he." The wesleyan Wesley reads it as no promise of a saving sight: the house is "irrecoverably consigned to desolation," and they shall not see him "till… taught by your calamities, ye shall be ready… to say, Blessed is he" — yet this "does not imply that they should then see Jesus at all," only that in their extremity they would long for the Messiah. The fathers gathered above divide: Theophylact refers it to the cross — "when you have crucified Me, you shall no more see Me until I come again" — while Augustine and Cyril refer it to the second coming, "when He should appear in glory," the Jews then "unwillingly" confessing him Lord; Bede conditions it on repentance — "unless you have worked repentance… you shall not see My face at the second coming." The shared and grounded note across the witnesses is the sentence of desolation upon the abandoned house, joined to a veiled word about a future recognition — whether at the entry, the cross, or the last day — that the abandoned city would one day, willingly or unwillingly, own him blessed who comes in the name of the Lord.
